Routing

Outpost uses file-based routing — every URL maps to a template file. There is no route registration API. Understanding the routing algorithm helps you add new pages, troubleshoot 404s, and extend the system with custom PHP when needed.

How routing works

Outpost does not have a route registration system like Laravel, Express, or WordPress. You do not define routes in engine.php or any config file. Instead, the router (front-router.php) maps incoming URLs to .html template files in the active theme directory.

Key concept: To create a new route, create a new .html file in your theme. The filename becomes the URL path. No PHP code or configuration is needed.

The routing algorithm

When a request arrives, front-router.php evaluates these rules in order. The first match wins.

PriorityRuleExample
1 Static files — If the URL matches a real file on disk (CSS, JS, images, fonts), serve it directly. PHP files are excluded. /assets/style.css → serves the file
2 /outpost/ paths — Admin panel, API, docs, and CMS internals. Served directly from the outpost/ directory. /outpost/api.php?action=pages
3 Sitemap — /sitemap.xml is handled by sitemap.php. /sitemap.xml
4 Lodge (member portal) — If the URL starts with the Lodge slug (default: /lodge), routes to Lodge templates: lodge/dashboard.html, lodge/edit.html, lodge/create.html, lodge/profile.html. /lodge/edit/42 → lodge/edit.html
5 Direct page match — URL path maps to {page}.html in the theme. The root URL (/) maps to index.html. /about → about.html
/ → index.html
6 Collection URL pattern — Checks each collection's url_pattern (e.g. /post/{slug}). If the URL matches, sets $_GET['slug'] and renders {collection-slug}.html (fallback: post.html). /post/my-article → post.html with $_GET['slug'] = 'my-article'
7 Channel URL pattern — Same as collections but for channels (community content). /listing/my-item → listing.html
8 404 — No match found. Renders 404.html from the theme if it exists, otherwise a plain HTML 404. /nonexistent → 404.html

Adding a new route

To add a route for /services, create a file at themes/your-theme/services.html:

<!-- themes/your-theme/services.html -->
<outpost-include partial="head"></outpost-include>
<outpost-include partial="nav"></outpost-include>

<main>
  <h1 data-outpost="page_title">Our Services</h1>
  <div data-outpost="page_body" data-type="richtext">
    <p>Default content here...</p>
  </div>
</main>

<outpost-include partial="footer"></outpost-include>

That's it. Visit /services and the page renders. The admin will automatically detect the new template's fields so you can edit them.

No restart needed. The router checks the filesystem on every request. New .html files are picked up immediately.

Custom PHP routes (standalone scripts)

For advanced use cases — custom API endpoints, webhook handlers, form processors, or pages with server-side logic — you can write a standalone PHP file that bootstraps the Outpost engine and renders a template.

When to use this: Only when you need server-side PHP logic that can't be achieved with the template engine alone. For most pages, just create an .html template file.

Complete example: custom PHP page

This example creates a /search endpoint that queries the database and renders results through a theme template.

Step 1: Create the PHP handler at search.php in your site root (next to index.php):

<?php
/**
 * Custom search page — standalone PHP that bootstraps Outpost
 * URL: /search?q=keyword
 */

// 1. Bootstrap Outpost (load config, database, and engine)
$outpostDir = __DIR__ . '/outpost';
require_once $outpostDir . '/config.php';
require_once $outpostDir . '/db.php';
require_once $outpostDir . '/engine.php';

// 2. Detect the active theme and template engine version
$themeRow    = OutpostDB::fetchOne("SELECT value FROM settings WHERE key = 'active_theme'");
$activeTheme = ($themeRow && $themeRow['value']) ? $themeRow['value'] : 'starter';
$themeDir    = OUTPOST_THEMES_DIR . $activeTheme;

$useV2 = outpost_detect_engine_version($themeDir);
if ($useV2) {
    require_once $outpostDir . '/template-engine-v2.php';
} else {
    require_once $outpostDir . '/template-engine.php';
}

// 3. Your custom logic
$query   = trim($_GET['q'] ?? '');
$results = [];

if ($query !== '') {
    // Search collection items by title
    $results = OutpostDB::fetchAll(
        "SELECT ci.slug, ci.data, c.slug AS collection_slug, c.url_pattern
         FROM collection_items ci
         JOIN collections c ON c.id = ci.collection_id
         WHERE ci.status = 'published'
           AND json_extract(ci.data, '$.title') LIKE ?
         ORDER BY ci.published_at DESC
         LIMIT 20",
        ['%' . $query . '%']
    );
}

// 4. Make data available to the template via a global
$GLOBALS['_outpost_search_query']   = htmlspecialchars($query);
$GLOBALS['_outpost_search_results'] = $results;

// 5. Initialize and render
outpost_init();

$templateFile = $themeDir . '/search.html';
if (!file_exists($templateFile)) {
    http_response_code(404);
    echo '<h1>Search template not found</h1>';
    exit;
}

outpost_render_template($templateFile, $themeDir);

Step 2: Create the template at themes/your-theme/search.html:

<outpost-include partial="head"></outpost-include>
<outpost-include partial="nav"></outpost-include>

<main>
  <h1>Search</h1>
  <form action="/search" method="get">
    <input type="text" name="q" placeholder="Search...">
    <button type="submit">Search</button>
  </form>
  <!-- Results are rendered by the PHP handler above -->
</main>

<outpost-include partial="footer"></outpost-include>

Step 3: Route the URL. Add a rewrite rule so /search hits your PHP file:

# Apache (.htaccess) — add BEFORE the catch-all rule
RewriteRule ^search$ search.php [L]

# Nginx — add BEFORE the try_files block
location = /search {
    fastcgi_pass unix:/run/php/php-fpm.sock;
    fastcgi_param SCRIPT_FILENAME $document_root/search.php;
    include fastcgi_params;
}

# PHP dev server — add a check in front-router.php (priority 2.5):
# if ($path === '/search') { require $docRoot . '/search.php'; return true; }

Bootstrap reference

The minimum code to bootstrap Outpost from a standalone PHP file:

<?php
// Minimum bootstrap — gives you database + engine + template rendering
$outpostDir = __DIR__ . '/outpost';
require_once $outpostDir . '/config.php';     // Constants: OUTPOST_DIR, OUTPOST_DB_PATH, etc.
require_once $outpostDir . '/db.php';          // OutpostDB class (static PDO wrapper)
require_once $outpostDir . '/engine.php';      // Template helper functions + outpost_init()

// Resolve theme
$themeRow    = OutpostDB::fetchOne("SELECT value FROM settings WHERE key = 'active_theme'");
$activeTheme = ($themeRow && $themeRow['value']) ? $themeRow['value'] : 'starter';
$themeDir    = OUTPOST_THEMES_DIR . $activeTheme;

// Load template engine (v2 data-attributes or v1 Liquid)
$useV2 = outpost_detect_engine_version($themeDir);
require_once $outpostDir . ($useV2 ? '/template-engine-v2.php' : '/template-engine.php');

// Initialize (runs migrations, loads fields, etc.)
outpost_init();

// Render any template
outpost_render_template($themeDir . '/my-page.html', $themeDir);
FileWhat it provides
config.phpConstants: OUTPOST_DIR, OUTPOST_DB_PATH, OUTPOST_THEMES_DIR, etc.
db.phpOutpostDB — static PDO wrapper for SQLite queries
engine.phpTemplate helper functions (cms_text(), cms_collection_list(), etc.), outpost_init(), outpost_render_template()
template-engine.phpOutpostTemplate — v1 Liquid compiler
template-engine-v2.phpOutpostTemplate — v2 data-attribute compiler
members.phpOutpostMember — member session checking (optional, for gated pages)

Dev server vs. production

The PHP built-in server (php -S localhost:8000 -t . front-router.php) and a production Apache/Nginx server both route through the same PHP logic, but with subtle differences:

Static file handling

On the dev server, the router script is called for every request including static files. The router checks whether the request maps to a real file and returns false to tell PHP to serve it natively. On production Apache/Nginx, the server handles static files before PHP is invoked — PHP never sees those requests.

REQUEST_URI reliability

On the PHP built-in server, $_SERVER['SCRIPT_FILENAME'] may point to the router script itself, making it unreliable. Outpost uses $_SERVER['REQUEST_URI'] for path-based decisions instead, which works consistently in both environments.

Apache .htaccess

RewriteEngine On
RewriteCond %{REQUEST_FILENAME} !-f
RewriteCond %{REQUEST_FILENAME} !-d
RewriteRule ^ index.php [L]

Nginx

location / {
 try_files $uri $uri/ /index.php?$query_string;
}
Tip — When troubleshooting routing issues on the dev server, check the PHP built-in server output in your terminal. It logs every request with status codes. A 404 for a file that should exist is usually a path issue.
